Re:
Fnrn Polrrrcer- PRACTTcEs ConMrssroN
,128 J Street r Suite 620 e Sacramento. CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 r Fax (916) 322-0886
February 2,2009
Bruce Gibson
Supervisor, District 2
San Luis Obispo County ,
1055 Monterey, Room D430
San Luis Obispo, CA 93408-1003
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. I-09-001
Dear Mr. Gibson:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").' Because it appears that material facts
provided in your request are incomplete, we treat your request as one for informal assistance,
rather than formal advice. lnformal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity
provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)
Also, please note that our informal assistance is based solely on the provisions of the
Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws
such as common law conflict of interest or Government Code Section 1090. In addition, the
Commission will not advise with respect to past conduct. (Regulation 18329(bX8)(A).)
Therefore, nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that may have
already taken place, (including conduct alleged in our Enforcement Case No. 07-0770), and any
conclusions contained in this letter apply only to prospective actions.
QUESTION
May you, as a member of the county board of supervisors, participate in a decision
regarding a subdivision and land use permit application by a company solely owned by an
individual where another company solely owned by this individual owns a 25 percent interest in
a company in which the 75 percent owner is a company in which your spoLlse has a 0.82 percent
ownership interest?
I The Political Refirrm Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
ref'erences are to the Government Code. unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections I 8 I l0 through I 8997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. AII
regulatory references are to'fitle 2. Division 6 of the Calitbrnia Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.


File No. I-09-001
Page No. 2
CONCLUSION
You may not participate in the decision if the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect on your economic interests. [f there is shared management and control
of the company (that is not the applicant) that is solely owned by the individual and the company
in which he owns a 25 percent interest (and in which your spouse has an economic interest), you
may have an economic interest in both companies, and if the materiality standards for all of the
companies are met, you may have a disqualifying conflict of interest. As indicated below, we do
not have sufficient facts to make this determination.
FACTS
The San Luis Obispo County Board of Supervisors is considering an application by Santa
Margarita Ranch, LLC regarding a subdivision and land use permit. Rob Rossi is the sole owner
of Santa Margarita Ranch, LLC. Mr. Rossi is also the sole owner of Exposition Park, LLC.
Exposition Park, LLC owns a 25 percent interest in SLOPHA Z,LLC. The other 75 percent is
owned by SLOPHA I, LLC. Mr. Rossi serves as one of four managing members of SLOPHA 2.
The other three managing members are members of SLOPHA I's executive committee.
SLOPHA I is owned by approximately 90 members and is governed by a board of 13 members
and an executive committee of five members. Your spouse is one of the 90 members of
SLOPHA l, with an interest of approximately 0.82 percent. Her investment in SLOPHA I is
$2,000 or more and she does not serve on its board or its executive committee. She has no
ownership interest in and holds no positions with SLOPHA 2, Exposition Park, or Santa
Margarita Ranch.
AI\ALYSIS
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will "perform their
duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the
financial interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).) Section 87100
prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her
official position to influence a govemmental decision in which the official has a financial
interest.
The Commission has adopted an eighrstep standard analysis for deciding whether an
official has a disqualifying conflict of interest. (Regulation 18700(b).) The general rule,
however, is that a conflict of interest exists whenever a public official makes a govemmental
decision that has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of his or her
financial interests.


File No. I-09-001
Page No.3
Step 1. Are you a "public official" within the meaning of section 87f00?
Section 82048 defines a public official as "every member, officer, employee or consultant
of a state or local govemment agency." As a member of the county board of supervisor, which is
a local government agency, you are a public official. Therefore, you may not make, participate
in making, or otherwise use your position to make, participate in making or influence any
decisions that will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on any of your
economic interests.2
Step 2. Will you be making, participating in making or influencing a governmental
decision?
A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting within the
authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or commits his or her
agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her
agency. (Regulation 18702.1.) A public official "participates in a govemmental decision" when,
acting within the authority of his or her position and without significant substantive or
intervening review, the official negotiates, advises, or makes recommendations to the
decisionmaker regarding the governmental decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) A public official is
attempting to use his or her official position to influence a governmental decision if, for the
purpose of influencing the decision, the official contacts or appears before or otherwise attempts
to influence, any member, officer, employee, or consultant of his or her agency. (Regulation
18702.3.)
By participating in a county board of supervisors vote on Santa Margarita Ranch's
application, you will be making a govemmental decision.
Step 3. What are your economic interests?
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to conflicts of interest arising
from certain enumerated economic interests. These economic interests are described in Section
87103 and Regulations 18703-18703.5, inclusive:
o A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he
or she has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(a); Regulation
18703.1(a).)
2 When a public official who holds an office specified in section 87200 has a conf'lict of interest in a
decision noticed at a public meeting, then he or she must: ( I ) immediately prior to the discussion of the item, orally
identify each type of economic interest involved in the decision as well as details of the economic interest. as
discussed in regulation 18702.5(b), on the record of the meeting: (2) recuse himself or herself; and (3) leave the
room for the duration of the discussion and,/or vote on the item. For closed sessions, consent calendars, absences
and speaking as a member of the public regarding personal interests, special rules found in regulation 18702.5(c)
and 18702.5(d) apply.


File No. I-09-001
Page No.4
o A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or
she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management.
(Section 87103(d); Regulation 18703. 1(b).)
o A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she
has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b); Regulation L8703.2.)
o An official has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income, totaling $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section
87 103(c); Regulation I 8703.3.)
o A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her
if the gifts total $420 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Secrion 87103(e);
Regulation 18703.4.)
o A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses,
income, assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate family. This is commonly
referred to as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5.)
Investments
You have an indirect economic interest in SLOPHA I by virtue of your spouse's
investment worth $2,000 or more in the company.3 (Section AZ}y;Regulition iSZOf .tlay.;
You also have an economic interest in SLOPHA 2 because of SLOPHA I's 75 percent
ownership interest in SLOPHA 2. Under Regulation 18703.1(d)(2)(A), a public official has an
economic interest in a business entity controlled by another business entity in which the official
has an economic interest.
Whether you have an economic interest in Exposition Park, LLC depends on
whether Exposition Park and SLOPHA 2 are "otherwise related" business entities as
defined in Regulation 18703.1(dX2). At the outset, we wish to point out that this detailed
definition of what constitutes an "otherwise related" business entity can potentially set up
a "domino effect" of disqualifying conflicts of interest. For example, where various
business entities are inter-related, a potentially disqualifying conflict of interest may
apply to all entities.
SLOPHA 2 and Exposition Park are otherwise related business entities if (i) SLOPHAz
has a controlling ownership interesr in Exposition Park under Regulation 18703.1(dX2)(A); (ii)
there is shared management and control between SLOPHA 2 andExposition Park under
18703.1(d)(2XB); or (iii) a single controlling owner (50 percent or greater ownership) owns both
SLOPHA 2 and Exposition Park under Regulation 18703.1(d)(2)(C).
I Indirect investment means any investment or interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of a public
official (Section 87 103)


File No. I-09-001
Page No. 5
From Mr. Rossi's sole ownership of Exposition Park, it is obvious that SLOPHA 2 does
not have a controlling ownership interest in Exposition Park. (Regulation 18703.1(d)(2)(A).)
Because SLOPHA 2 is owned 25 percent by Exposition Park and 75 percent by
SLOPHA 1, the two companies do not share a common controlling owner. (Regulation
8703.1(dX2)(c).)
The facts you provided, however, sllggest that there may be "shared management
and control between the entities" under Regulation 18703.1(d)(2XB). The factors to be
considered in determining whether two entities share management and control are:
"(i) The same person or substantially the same person owns and manages
the two entities;
"(ii) There are common or commingled funds or assets;
"(iii) The business entities share the use of the same offices or employees,
or otherwise share activities, resources or personnel on a regular basis;
"(iv) There is otherwise a regular and close working relationship between
the entities." (Id.)
The facts indicate that Exposition Park and SLOPHA 2 are not owned and
managed by the same person or substantially the same person. (Exposition Park holds
only a 25 percent ownership interest in SLOPHA 2 and Exposition Park is solely owned
by Mr. Rossi.) Hence, the first factor is not met.
There is also no indication that SLOPHA 2 and Exposition Park commingle funds
or assets, nor do they share offices, employees, activities, resources or personnel on a
regular basis. Thus, the second and third factors are not met.
Finally, we consider the fourth factor, set forth in'Regulation
18703.1(d)(2XB)(iv), which requires that we examine the relationship between
Exposition Park and SLOPHA 2 to see if the relationship is a "regular and close working
relationship."4 'W'e believe we need additional facts to urderstand the relationship
between Mr. Rossi/Exposition Park and SLOPHA 2.
For example, we know that there is a relationship between Exposition Park and
SLOPHA 2 rnthat Exposition Park owns 25 percent of SLOPHA 2 and, Rob Rossi, the
sole owner of Exposition Park, serves as one of four managing members of SLOPHA 2
Under these circumstances, there may be a "regular and close working relationship"
I For conflict-of-interest purposes. the Commission has "pierced" through entities. such as corporations, to
consider the nature of the relationship between the entity and the person or persons who control the entity. 1ln re
Lumsdon(1976) 2FPPCOps. l40l Inre Kalm(1976) 2FPPCOps. l5l.) ApplyingthisprincipletoMr.Rossi,his
interests are deemed to be the same as those of Exposition Park.


File No. I-09-001
Page No. 6
between SLOPHA 2 and Mr. Rossi/Exposition Park. In the Krause /Il Advice Letter, No.
A-97-572, we advised that even though one partner had controlling ownership and was
the manager of the entity, the other partner nevertheless had a regular and cloie working
relationship with the entity because the relationship included joint decisions on the
overall management of the entity's project as well as financing and marketing of the
project.
Without additional information regarding the relationship between SLOpHA 2
and Mr. Rossi/Exposition Park, we are unable to determine if there is shared management
and control under Regulation 18703.1(d)(2XB) that would render Exposition park an
"otherwise related entity" of SLOPHA 2. We continue our analysis, issuming this to be
the case, so that you may apply this analysis in the event that you obtain information
indicating that there is shared management and control between SLOpHA 2 and
Mr. Rossi/Exposition Park. If there is shared management and control under Regulation
18703.1(d)(2)(B), you would have an economic interest in SLOHA 2 andExposition
Park. If you have an economic interest in Exposition Park, you would have an economic
interest in santa Margarita Ranch, an "otherwise affiliated entity', by virtue of
Mr. Rossi's sole ownership of both entities. (Regularion lg703.l(dX2)(B).)
Sources of Income
The facts indicate that you may also have an economic interest in SLOpHA I as a source
of income. An official has an economic interest in any source of income, including promised
income, totaling $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section SZIO:(g);
Regulation 18703.3.) Your spouse received $3,500 in returned capital from SLOpHA l. If she
received this payment within 12 months of your governmental decision regarding Santa
Margarita Ranch's application, you will have an economic interest in SLOPHA I as a source of
income. The Act's definition of income (Section 82030) is very broad with few exceptions.
While under tax laws, a return of capital may not always be considered income, undei the Act,
such a payment is income and includes any community property interest in the income of your
spouse.
You may also have an economic interest in clients or any other sources of income of
SLOPHA l. Any such sources of income would be income to you within the meaning of Section
87103(c). Your facts do not suggest that any of the entities you have described are sources of
income to SLOPHA l.
Finally, under Regulation 18703.3, which addresses sources of income, an official has an
economic interest in a business entity that is "otherwise related" to the business entity in which
the official has a source of income economic interest under Section 87103(c). This provision
reinforces our analysis above regarding your possible economic interests in SLOpHA 2,
Exposition Park and Santa Margarita Ranch.


File No. I-09-001
Page No. 7
Step 4. Will your economic interests be directly or indirectly involved in decisions
you will make, participate in making or influence as a public official?
A person, including business entities and sources of income, is directly involved in a
decision before an official's agency when that person, either directly or by an agent (l) initiates
the proceeding by filing an application, claim, appeal or similar request; or (2) is a named party
in, or is the subject of, the proceeding. (Regulation 18704.1.) Because neither SLOPHA 1 nor
SLOPHA 2 are initiating the proceeding for the application that is the subject of the government
decision nor are they named parties or the subjects of the proceeding, your economic interest in
these entities is not directly involved in the decision regarding the application. When an
economic interest is not directly involved in a govemmental decision, it is considered indirectly
involved.
However, by virtue of the "piercing" principle discussed above, your possible economic
interest in Exposition Park/Mr. Rossi results in an economic interest in Santa Margarita
//lVlr. Rossi. Your economic interest in Mr. Rossi and Santa Margarita Ranch are directly
involved in the application because Santa Margarita Ranch, which is solely owned by Mr. Rossi,
is the applicant in the subject govemmental decision.
Step 5. What is the applicable materiality standard?
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial
effect of a governmental decision on a public official's economic interest is material.
(Regulation I 8700(a).)
SLOPHA l. SLOPHA 2 and Exposition Park: lndirectly Involved
Regulation 18705.3(b) states that for sources of income that are business entities, the
same materiality standards applicable to business entities under Regulation 18705.1(c) apply.
Regulation 18705.1(c) provides different materiality standards for an indirectly involved
business based upon the financial size of the business. Although you have not provided this
information, we will provide an example so that you may apply our analysis once you obtain the
relevant facts.s If SLOPHA l, SLOPHA 2 anddxposition 
-Park 
are small businesses (net income
of less than $500,000 per year), the financial effect of the governmental decision is material if it
is reasonably foreseeable that:
"(A) The govemmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in
the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the amount of
$20,000 or more; or
"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity incurring
or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing
expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 or more; or
s See complete list of financial criteria in Regulation 18705. l(cX3), copy enclosed. If those financial
criteria are not reached, the criteria in Regulation I 8705. I (cX4) apply.


File No. I-09-001
Page No. 8
"(c) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in
the value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of $20,000 or more."
(Regulation 1 8705. I (c)(4XA-C).)"
Santa Margarita Ranch: Directl)r lnvolved
The general rule under Regulation 18705.1(b)(1) is that "the financial effect of a
governmental decision on a business entity which is directly involved in the governmental
decision is presumed to be material." (Emphasis added.)
Regulation 18705.1(b)(2) provides an exception to the general rule where the public
official's only economic interest in the business entity is an investment interest, the public
official's investment interest in the business entity is worth $25,000 or less, and the business
entity's before-tax eamings are $2.5 million or more. Your facts indicate that both of the first
two factors are met. Therefore, your economic interest in Santa Margarita Ranch would be
material only if Santa Margarita Ranch's before-tax earnings for its most recent fiscal year were
$2.5 million or more.
Step Six: Are the material financial effects reasonably foreseeable?
Once a public official has determined the materiality standard that applies to his or her
economic interest, the next step is determining whether it is "reasonably foreseeable" that
the standard will be met. A financial effect on an economic interest is considered
"reasonably foreseeable" if there is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation
18706(a).) A financial effect need not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable,
but it must be more than a mere possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FppC ops. 198.)
The responsibility for making this final determination rests with the public
official, considering the facts as they exist at the time of the decision. The Commission
does not act as a finder of fact when providing advice. (In re Oglesby. (1975) I FPPC
Ops.71.)
In making your determination as to whether it is reasonably foreseeable that a decision
regarding Santa Margarita Ranch's application will have a material financial effect on
SLOPHA l, SLOPHA 2, and Exposition Park, it may be helpful to consider that the Commission
has opined that a financial effect is not reasonably foreseeable when there exists an "extra degree
of remoteness" between a particular govemmental decision and a financial effect upon an
official's economic interests. (In re Thorner, supra; Esselstein Advice Letter, No. I-03-293.)
Steps 7 & 8: The "public generally" and "legally required participation"
exceptions.
Even if a material financial effect on a public official's economic interest is reasonably
foreseeable, he or she still may not be disqualified if the financial effect of the governmental
decision on the public official's economic interest is indistinguishable from its effect on the


File No. I-09-001
Page No. 9
public generally (Section 87103, Regulations 18700(b)(7) and 18707(a)), or if the official is
legally required to participate (Section 87103; Regulation 18708). You have not presented any
facts indicating that either of these exceptions is applicable to your situation.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
By: Valentina Joyce
Counsel, Legal Division
vJjgl
Enclosure

sections.question:
May you, as a member of the county board of supervisors, participate in a decision
regarding a subdivision and land use permit application by a company solely owned by an
individual where another company solely owned by this individual owns a 25 percent interest in
a company in which the 75 percent owner is a company in which your spoLlse has a 0.82 percent
ownership interest?
I The Political Refirrm Act is contained inAII
regulatory references are to'fitle 2. Division 6 of the Calitbrnia Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.
sections.conclusion:
You may not participate in the decision if the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect on your economic interests. [f there is shared management and control
of the company (that is not the applicant) that is solely owned by the individual and the company
in which he owns a 25 percent interest (and in which your spouse has an economic interest), you
may have an economic interest in both companies, and if the materiality standards for all of the
companies are met, you may have a disqualifying conflict of interest. As indicated below, we do
not have sufficient facts to make this determination.
sections.facts:
The San Luis Obispo County Board of Supervisors is considering an application by Santa
Margarita Ranch, LLC regarding a subdivision and land use permit. Rob Rossi is the sole owner
of Santa Margarita Ranch, LLC. Mr. Rossi is also the sole owner of Exposition Park, LLC.
Exposition Park, LLC owns a 25 percent interest in SLOPHA Z,LLC. The other 75 percent is
owned by SLOPHA I, LLC. Mr. Rossi serves as one of four managing members of SLOPHA 2.
The other three managing members are members of SLOPHA I's executive committee.
SLOPHA I is owned by approximately 90 members and is governed by a board of 13 members
and an executive committee of five members. Your spouse is one of the 90 members of
SLOPHA l, with an interest of approximately 0.82 percent. Her investment in SLOPHA I is
$2,000 or more and she does not serve on its board or its executive committee. She has no
ownership interest in and holds no positions with SLOPHA 2, Exposition Park, or Santa
Margarita Ranch.
sections.analysis:
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will "perform their
duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the
financial interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).) Section 87100
prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her
official position to influence a govemmental decision in which the official has a financial
interest.
The Commission has adopted an eighrstep standard analysis for deciding whether an
official has a disqualifying conflict of interest. (Regulation 18700(b).) The general rule,
however, is that a conflict of interest exists whenever a public official makes a govemmental
decision that has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of his or her
financial interests.
Step 1. Are you a "public official" within the meaning of section 87f00?
Section 82048 defines a public official as "every member, officer, employee or consultant
of a state or local govemment agency." As a member of the county board of supervisor, which is
a local government agency, you are a public official. Therefore, you may not make, participate
in making, or otherwise use your position to make, participate in making or influence any
decisions that will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on any of your
economic interests.2
Step 2. Will you be making, participating in making or influencing a governmental
decision?
A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting within the
authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or commits his or her
agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her
agency. (Regulation 18702.1.) A public official "participates in a govemmental decision" when,
acting within the authority of his or her position and without significant substantive or
intervening review, the official negotiates, advises, or makes recommendations to the
decisionmaker regarding the governmental decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) A public official is
attempting to use his or her official position to influence a governmental decision if, for the
purpose of influencing the decision, the official contacts or appears before or otherwise attempts
to influence, any member, officer, employee, or consultant of his or her agency. (Regulation
18702.3.)
By participating in a county board of supervisors vote on Santa Margarita Ranch's
application, you will be making a govemmental decision.
Step 3. What are your economic interests?
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only to conflicts of interest arising
from certain enumerated economic interests. These economic interests are described in Section
87103 and Regulations 18703-18703.5, inclusive:
o A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he
or she has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(a); Regulation
18703.1(a).)
2 When a public official who holds an office specified in section 87200 has a conf'lict of interest in a
decision noticed at a public meeting, then he or she must: ( I ) immediately prior to the discussion of the item, orally
identify each type of economic interest involved in the decision as well as details of the economic interest. as
discussed in regulation 18702.5(b), on the record of the meeting: (2) recuse himself or herself; and (3) leave the
room for the duration of the discussion and,/or vote on the item. For closed sessions, consent calendars, absences
and speaking as a member of the public regarding personal interests, special rules found in regulation 18702.5(c)
and 18702.5(d) apply.
o A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or
she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management.
(Section 87103(d); Regulation 18703. 1(b).)
o A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she
has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b); Regulation L8703.2.)
o An official has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income, totaling $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section
87 103(c); Regulation I 8703.3.)
o A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her
if the gifts total $420 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Secrion 87103(e);
Regulation 18703.4.)
o A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses,
income, assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate family. This is commonly
referred to as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5.)
Investments
You have an indirect economic interest in SLOPHA I by virtue of your spouse's
investment worth $2,000 or more in the company.3 (Section AZ}y;Regulition iSZOf .tlay.;
You also have an economic interest in SLOPHA 2 because of SLOPHA I's 75 percent
ownership interest in SLOPHA 2. Under Regulation 18703.1(d)(2)(A), a public official has an
economic interest in a business entity controlled by another business entity in which the official
has an economic interest.
Whether you have an economic interest in Exposition Park, LLC depends on
whether Exposition Park and SLOPHA 2 are "otherwise related" business entities as
defined in Regulation 18703.1(dX2). At the outset, we wish to point out that this detailed
definition of what constitutes an "otherwise related" business entity can potentially set up
a "domino effect" of disqualifying conflicts of interest. For example, where various
business entities are inter-related, a potentially disqualifying conflict of interest may
apply to all entities.
SLOPHA 2 and Exposition Park are otherwise related business entities if (i) SLOPHAz
has a controlling ownership interesr in Exposition Park under Regulation 18703.1(dX2)(A); (ii)
there is shared management and control between SLOPHA 2 andExposition Park under
18703.1(d)(2XB); or (iii) a single controlling owner (50 percent or greater ownership) owns both
SLOPHA 2 and Exposition Park under Regulation 18703.1(d)(2)(C).
I Indirect investment means any investment or interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of a public
official (Section 87 103)
From Mr. Rossi's sole ownership of Exposition Park, it is obvious that SLOPHA 2 does
not have a controlling ownership interest in Exposition Park. (Regulation 18703.1(d)(2)(A).)
Because SLOPHA 2 is owned 25 percent by Exposition Park and 75 percent by
SLOPHA 1, the two companies do not share a common controlling owner. (Regulation
8703.1(dX2)(c).)
The facts you provided, however, sllggest that there may be "shared management
and control between the entities" under Regulation 18703.1(d)(2XB). The factors to be
considered in determining whether two entities share management and control are:
"(i) The same person or substantially the same person owns and manages
the two entities;
"(ii) There are common or commingled funds or assets;
"(iii) The business entities share the use of the same offices or employees,
or otherwise share activities, resources or personnel on a regular basis;
"(iv) There is otherwise a regular and close working relationship between
the entities." (Id.)
The facts indicate that Exposition Park and SLOPHA 2 are not owned and
managed by the same person or substantially the same person. (Exposition Park holds
only a 25 percent ownership interest in SLOPHA 2 and Exposition Park is solely owned
by Mr. Rossi.) Hence, the first factor is not met.
There is also no indication that SLOPHA 2 and Exposition Park commingle funds
or assets, nor do they share offices, employees, activities, resources or personnel on a
regular basis. Thus, the second and third factors are not met.
Finally, we consider the fourth factor, set forth in'Regulation
18703.1(d)(2XB)(iv), which requires that we examine the relationship between
Exposition Park and SLOPHA 2 to see if the relationship is a "regular and close working
relationship."4 'W'e believe we need additional facts to urderstand the relationship
between Mr. Rossi/Exposition Park and SLOPHA 2.
For example, we know that there is a relationship between Exposition Park and
SLOPHA 2 rnthat Exposition Park owns 25 percent of SLOPHA 2 and, Rob Rossi, the
sole owner of Exposition Park, serves as one of four managing members of SLOPHA 2
Under these circumstances, there may be a "regular and close working relationship"
I For conflict-of-interest purposes. the Commission has "pierced" through entities. such as corporations, to
consider the nature of the relationship between the entity and the person or persons who control the entity. 1ln re
Lumsdon(1976) 2FPPCOps. l40l Inre Kalm(1976) 2FPPCOps. l5l.) ApplyingthisprincipletoMr.Rossi,his
interests are deemed to be the same as those of Exposition Park.
between SLOPHA 2 and Mr. Rossi/Exposition Park. In the Krause /Il Advice Letter, No.
A-97-572, we advised that even though one partner had controlling ownership and was
the manager of the entity, the other partner nevertheless had a regular and cloie working
relationship with the entity because the relationship included joint decisions on the
overall management of the entity's project as well as financing and marketing of the
project.
Without additional information regarding the relationship between SLOpHA 2
and Mr. Rossi/Exposition Park, we are unable to determine if there is shared management
and control under Regulation 18703.1(d)(2XB) that would render Exposition park an
"otherwise related entity" of SLOPHA 2. We continue our analysis, issuming this to be
the case, so that you may apply this analysis in the event that you obtain information
indicating that there is shared management and control between SLOpHA 2 and
Mr. Rossi/Exposition Park. If there is shared management and control under Regulation
18703.1(d)(2)(B), you would have an economic interest in SLOHA 2 andExposition
Park. If you have an economic interest in Exposition Park, you would have an economic
interest in santa Margarita Ranch, an "otherwise affiliated entity', by virtue of
Mr. Rossi's sole ownership of both entities. (Regularion lg703.l(dX2)(B).)
Sources of Income
The facts indicate that you may also have an economic interest in SLOpHA I as a source
of income. An official has an economic interest in any source of income, including promised
income, totaling $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section SZIO:(g);
Regulation 18703.3.) Your spouse received $3,500 in returned capital from SLOpHA l. If she
received this payment within 12 months of your governmental decision regarding Santa
Margarita Ranch's application, you will have an economic interest in SLOPHA I as a source of
income. The Act's definition of income (Section 82030) is very broad with few exceptions.
While under tax laws, a return of capital may not always be considered income, undei the Act,
such a payment is income and includes any community property interest in the income of your
spouse.
You may also have an economic interest in clients or any other sources of income of
SLOPHA l. Any such sources of income would be income to you within the meaning of Section
87103(c). Your facts do not suggest that any of the entities you have described are sources of
income to SLOPHA l.
Finally, under Regulation 18703.3, which addresses sources of income, an official has an
economic interest in a business entity that is "otherwise related" to the business entity in which
the official has a source of income economic interest under Section 87103(c). This provision
reinforces our analysis above regarding your possible economic interests in SLOpHA 2,
Exposition Park and Santa Margarita Ranch.
Step 4. Will your economic interests be directly or indirectly involved in decisions
you will make, participate in making or influence as a public official?
A person, including business entities and sources of income, is directly involved in a
decision before an official's agency when that person, either directly or by an agent (l) initiates
the proceeding by filing an application, claim, appeal or similar request; or (2) is a named party
in, or is the subject of, the proceeding. (Regulation 18704.1.) Because neither SLOPHA 1 nor
SLOPHA 2 are initiating the proceeding for the application that is the subject of the government
decision nor are they named parties or the subjects of the proceeding, your economic interest in
these entities is not directly involved in the decision regarding the application. When an
economic interest is not directly involved in a govemmental decision, it is considered indirectly
involved.
However, by virtue of the "piercing" principle discussed above, your possible economic
interest in Exposition Park/Mr. Rossi results in an economic interest in Santa Margarita
//lVlr. Rossi. Your economic interest in Mr. Rossi and Santa Margarita Ranch are directly
involved in the application because Santa Margarita Ranch, which is solely owned by Mr. Rossi,
is the applicant in the subject govemmental decision.
Step 5. What is the applicable materiality standard?
A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial
effect of a governmental decision on a public official's economic interest is material.
(Regulation I 8700(a).)
SLOPHA l. SLOPHA 2 and Exposition Park: lndirectly Involved
Regulation 18705.3(b) states that for sources of income that are business entities, the
same materiality standards applicable to business entities under Regulation 18705.1(c) apply.
Regulation 18705.1(c) provides different materiality standards for an indirectly involved
business based upon the financial size of the business. Although you have not provided this
information, we will provide an example so that you may apply our analysis once you obtain the
relevant facts.s If SLOPHA l, SLOPHA 2 anddxposition 
-Park 
are small businesses (net income
of less than $500,000 per year), the financial effect of the governmental decision is material if it
is reasonably foreseeable that:
"(A) The govemmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in
the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the amount of
$20,000 or more; or
"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity incurring
or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing
expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 or more; or
s See complete list of financial criteria in Regulation 18705. l(cX3), copy enclosed. If those financial
criteria are not reached, the criteria in Regulation I 8705. I (cX4) apply.
"(c) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in
the value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of $20,000 or more."
(Regulation 1 8705. I (c)(4XA-C).)"
Santa Margarita Ranch: Directl)r lnvolved
The general rule under Regulation 18705.1(b)(1) is that "the financial effect of a
governmental decision on a business entity which is directly involved in the governmental
decision is presumed to be material." (Emphasis added.)
Regulation 18705.1(b)(2) provides an exception to the general rule where the public
official's only economic interest in the business entity is an investment interest, the public
official's investment interest in the business entity is worth $25,000 or less, and the business
entity's before-tax eamings are $2.5 million or more. Your facts indicate that both of the first
two factors are met. Therefore, your economic interest in Santa Margarita Ranch would be
material only if Santa Margarita Ranch's before-tax earnings for its most recent fiscal year were
$2.5 million or more.
Step Six: Are the material financial effects reasonably foreseeable?
Once a public official has determined the materiality standard that applies to his or her
economic interest, the next step is determining whether it is "reasonably foreseeable" that
the standard will be met. A financial effect on an economic interest is considered
"reasonably foreseeable" if there is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation
18706(a).) A financial effect need not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable,
but it must be more than a mere possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FppC ops. 198.)
The responsibility for making this final determination rests with the public
official, considering the facts as they exist at the time of the decision. The Commission
does not act as a finder of fact when providing advice. (In re Oglesby. (1975) I FPPC
Ops.71.)
In making your determination as to whether it is reasonably foreseeable that a decision
regarding Santa Margarita Ranch's application will have a material financial effect on
SLOPHA l, SLOPHA 2, and Exposition Park, it may be helpful to consider that the Commission
has opined that a financial effect is not reasonably foreseeable when there exists an "extra degree
of remoteness" between a particular govemmental decision and a financial effect upon an
official's economic interests. (In re Thorner, supra; Esselstein Advice Letter, No. I-03-293.)
Steps 7 & 8: The "public generally" and "legally required participation"
exceptions.
Even if a material financial effect on a public official's economic interest is reasonably
foreseeable, he or she still may not be disqualified if the financial effect of the governmental
decision on the public official's economic interest is indistinguishable from its effect on the
public generally (Section 87103, Regulations 18700(b)(7) and 18707(a)), or if the official is
legally required to participate (Section 87103; Regulation 18708). You have not presented any
facts indicating that either of these exceptions is applicable to your situation.

QUESTION: May you, as a member of the county board of supervisors, participate in a decision
regarding a subdivision and land use permit application by a company solely owned by an
individual where another company solely owned by this individual owns a 25 percent interest in
a company in which the 75 percent owner is a company in which your spoLlse has a 0.82 percent
ownership interest?
I The Political Refirrm Act is contained inAII
regulatory references are to'fitle 2. Division 6 of the Calitbrnia Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

CONCLUSION: You may not participate in the decision if the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect on your economic interests. [f there is shared management and control
of the company (that is not the applicant) that is solely owned by the individual and the company
in which he owns a 25 percent interest (and in which your spouse has an economic interest), you
may have an economic interest in both companies, and if the materiality standards for all of the
companies are met, you may have a disqualifying conflict of interest. As indicated below, we do
not have sufficient facts to make this determination.

CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 r Fax (916) 322-0886 February 2,2009 Bruce Gibson Supervisor, District 2 San Luis Obispo County , 1055 Monterey, Room D430 San Luis Obispo, CA 93408-1003 Your Request for Advice Our File No. I-09-001 Dear Mr. Gibson: This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").' Because it appears that material facts provided in your request are incomplete, we treat your request as one for informal assistance, rather than formal advice. lnformal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).) Also, please note that our informal assistance is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as common law conflict of interest or Government Code Section 1090. In addition, the Commission will not advise with respect to past conduct. (Regulation 18329(bX8)(A).) Therefore, nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that may have already taken place, (including conduct alleged in our Enforcement Case No. 07-0770), and any conclusions contained in this letter apply only to prospective actions. QUESTION May you, as a member of the county board of supervisors, participate in a decision regarding a subdivision and land use permit application by a company solely owned by an individual where another company solely owned by this individual owns a 25 percent interest in a company in which the 75 percent owner is a company in which your spoLlse has a 0.82 percent ownership interest? I The Political Refirrm Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory ref'erences are to the Government Code. unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections I 8 I l0 through I 8997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. AII regulatory references are to'fitle 2. Division 6 of the Calitbrnia Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. File No. I-09-001 Page No. 2 CONCLUSION You may not participate in the decision if the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on your economic interests. [f there is shared management and control of the company (that is not the applicant) that is solely owned by the individual and the company in which he owns a 25 percent interest (and in which your spouse has an economic interest), you may have an economic interest in both companies, and if the materiality standards for all of the companies are met, you may have a disqualifying conflict of interest. As indicated below, we do not have sufficient facts to make this determination. FACTS The San Luis Obispo County Board of Supervisors is considering an application by Santa Margarita Ranch, LLC regarding a subdivision and land use permit. Rob Rossi is the sole owner of Santa Margarita
